title: Kernel privilege and the no-safety-net rule
description: Kernel code runs with full hardware privilege and no process isolation, so a fault panics the machine and any unchecked input is a system-wide exploit.
body: Use when reasoning about why kernel bugs are categorically worse than user-space bugs. In kernel mode there is no segfault-and-continue: a bad dereference panics or corrupts the whole system, and code runs above all process protections, so an unvalidated user input can escalate privilege. Do not apply user-space habits (catch and recover, trust local callers) in the kernel. Actions: validate at every boundary, fail closed, and test destructively in a VM. Validation signals include sanitizer-clean runs and fault-injection survival. Risk boundary: a kernel defect can compromise or crash the entire machine; treat correctness and input validation here as security-critical and escalate uncertain cases.
